content:
## Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)

While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are traditionally associated with image processing, they serve as a notable alternative architecture to Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) for sequence modeling tasks. Unlike RNNs, which must process sequential data strictly step-by-step, CNNs apply filters across the input data. This localized filtering mechanism allows for a significant degree of parallelization during training and inference, as the computation of a filter at one position is largely independent of its computation at another position. 

However, this architectural advantage comes at a cost. Because a standard CNN computes its hidden representations by applying filters to local neighborhoods, it struggles to establish a truly global receptive field. Even with deep architectures, capturing relationships between distant elements in a sequence requires passing information through many layers, making it fundamentally less direct than self-attention mechanisms. The architectural trade-offs of utilizing CNNs for these types of tasks, particularly concerning their parallelization capabilities and receptive field limitations, form a foundational topic of study in the literature.

### Try it

Imagine you are designing a 1D CNN to classify long text documents (e.g., 10,000 tokens) into different categories. To keep the parameter count low and avoid massive computational overhead, you decide to use a network with only three convolutional layers and a kernel size of 3. 

Given the lack of a global receptive field in this architecture, explain how you might modify the network or your data processing pipeline to ensure the model can still capture the overall context (e.g., relating a key concept in the first paragraph to a conclusion in the last paragraph) without simply making the network hundreds of layers deep.
